text: Operative management of navicular stress fractures has a significantly higher success rate than conservative management (97.9% vs 78.1%, OR = 5.52, p = 0.004).
Refracture rate after conservative management of navicular stress fractures is significantly higher than after operative management (23.53% vs 1.28%, OR = 0.083, p = 0.047).

Mean return-to-play time for navicular stress fractures is approximately 4.17 months after operative management and 4.67 months after conservative management, with no significant difference between approaches.
Return-to-play rate following operative management of navicular stress fractures is 98.98%, compared to 73.43% for conservative management, though the difference is not statistically significant.
The mean duration of symptoms before diagnosis of navicular stress fractures is alarmingly long at 9.86 months (range 3.3–123.6 months), indicating frequent diagnostic delays.
Operative fixation is recommended for all navicular stress fractures (types I through III) in athletes due to superior success rates, lower refracture rates, and lower non-union rates.
CT imaging is strongly recommended as an adjunct in the workup of suspected navicular stress fractures due to the high rate of delayed or missed diagnoses.
